perturbation.original_text: Any dispute arising under or relating to this Agreement shall be resolved exclusively by arbitration under the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association, with the venue of any such arbitration proceeding to be in Santa Clara, California or such other location as maybe agreed by the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute arising under or relating to this Agreement shall be resolved exclusively under the governing law in Section 12(n).
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates arbitration for dispute resolution. By moving this clause to the 'Governing Law' section, it obscures the requirement for arbitration, making it less prominent and potentially overlooked. This can contradict legal requirements for clear and conspicuous arbitration agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 1281.2 outlines the process for enforcing arbitration agreements. By obscuring the arbitration agreement within the governing law section, its enforceability becomes questionable, especially if it does not meet the standard of a clear and conspicuous waiver of the right to a jury trial.

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281.2. On petition of a party to an arbitration agreement alleging the existence of a written agreement to arbitrate a controversy and that a party to the agreement refuses to arbitrate that controversy, the court shall order the petitioner and the respondent to arbitrate the controversy if it determines that an agreement to arbitrate the controversy exists, unless it determines that: (a) The right to compel arbitration has been waived by the petitioner; or (b) Grounds exist for rescission of the agreement. (c) A party to the arbitration agreement is also a party to a pending court action or special proceeding with a third party, arising out of the same transaction or series of related transactions and there is a possibility of conflicting rulings on a common issue of law or fact. For purposes of this section, a pending court action or special proceeding includes an action or proceeding initiated by the party refusing to arbitrate after the petit
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly mandates arbitration under specific rules. The changed text replaces this clear directive with a reference to the 'Governing Law' section, obscuring the arbitration requirement. This constitutes a structural flaw where a required legal disclosure is placed in a potentially misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret, as defined in the perturbation definitions. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation support this view, suggesting it could impact enforceability. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP § 1281.2, emphasizes the importance of a written agreement to arbitrate and the court's role in enforcing it. By obscuring this agreement, the changed text weakens the clarity and enforceability of the arbitration clause, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The award rendered by the arbitrator shall be final, shall identify a winning Party, and judgment may be entered upon the award in accordance with applicable law in any court having jurisdiction thereof
perturbation.changed_text: The award rendered by the arbitrator shall be determined by mutual agreement of the parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states that the arbitrator's decision is final. The modified text allows the parties to mutually agree to the award, thus negating the finality of the arbitration and rendering the process optional. This contradicts the binding nature typically associated with arbitration agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 9 U.S. Code § 9 - Award of arbitrators; confirmation; jurisdiction; proceeding
perturbation.law_explanation: 9 U.S.C. § 9 allows for the confirmation of arbitration awards and states that any court having jurisdiction may enter judgment upon this award. By including that the award will be determined by mutual agreement of the parties, this contradicts the purpose of arbitration.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 9 / CHAPTER 1 / § 9 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 9 USC 9 : Award of arbitrators; confirmation; jurisdiction; procedure Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 9-ARBITRATION CHAPTER 1-GENERAL PROVISIONS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous §9. Award of arbitrators; confirmation; jurisdiction; procedure If the parties in their agreement have agreed that a judgment of the court shall be entered upon the award made pursuant to the arbitration, and shall specify the court, then at any time within one year after the award is made any party to the arbitration may apply to the court so specified for an order confirming the award, and thereupon the court must grant such an order unless the awar
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the finality of an arbitrator's award with a requirement for mutual agreement between the parties. This contradicts the purpose of arbitration, which is to provide a binding resolution. 9 U.S.C. § 9 discusses the confirmation of arbitration awards and the jurisdiction of courts to enter judgment upon such awards, implying that the award is meant to be binding and enforceable, and not subject to mutual agreement after the arbitration process. The original text explicitly stated the award was 'final', and the perturbed text negates this fundamental aspect of arbitration.

perturbation.original_text: In addition, the winning Party's reasonable attorneys' fees and costs shall be paid by the non­winning Party.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition, each party shall bear their own attorneys' fees and costs.
perturbation.explanation: The original text awards attorneys' fees and costs to the winning party. The modification requires each party to bear their own costs regardless of the outcome. This undermines the incentive to pursue valid claims and contradicts statutes allowing for fee-shifting in certain contract disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1717 provides for the reciprocal award of attorneys' fees in contract actions where the contract provides for attorneys' fees to one party. The modification violates this principle by stating that each party must bear their own attorneys' fees.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1717 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1717. (a) In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs. Where a contract provides for attorney’s fees, as set forth above, that provision shall be construed as applying to the entire contract, unless each party was represented by counsel in the negotiation and execution of the contract, and the fact of that representation is specified in the contract. Reasonable attorney’s fees shall be fixed by the court, and shall be an element of the costs of suit. Attorney’s fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract which is entered into after the effective date of this section. Any provision in any such contract which provides for a waiver of
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts California Civil Code § 1717(a). The original text stipulated that the losing party pays the winning party's attorney fees, aligning with the statute's provision for awarding attorney's fees to the prevailing party when a contract so provides. The modified text, stating that each party bears their own fees, nullifies this provision and goes against the principle of reciprocal fee awards as described in the scraped law snippet, creating a clear contradiction. The snippet says "In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs."
